elements.sent-2411-001: Blockchain networks are facing increasingly heterogeneous computational demands, and in response, protocol designers have started building specialized infrastructure to supply that demand.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper introduces Resonance: a new kind of transaction fee mechanism that operates in a general two-sided marketplace setting with extreme preference heterogeneity on both sides of the market.
elements.sent-2411-003: We allow users submitting transactions to have arbitrary valuations for inclusion, nodes responsible for executing transactions to incur arbitrary net costs for executing any bundle, and further allow for arbitrary constraints in allocation validity.
elements.sent-2411-004: These constraints, for example, may range from representing an individual node's specialized hardware constraints to denoting the fact that transactions may not be executed in parallel across different nodes if they utilize the same part of the network's state.
elements.sent-2411-005: Transactions may even require multiple nodes for execution.
elements.sent-2411-006: Resonance's design utilizes competition among sophisticated brokers to find idiosyncratic prices.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that at pure Nash equilibria, Resonance finds an efficient outcome and minimizes the need for strategization by users and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-008: It is also budget-balanced, individually rational for all parties, and computationally tractable.
elements.sent-2411-009: Public blockchain networks utilize decentralized consensus protocols to provide a ``trusted'' computing environment; ideally, these protocols provide users practical assurances that transactions they submit to the network will be faithfully executed to modify a consistent and publicly-accessible network state.
elements.sent-2411-010: As more applications for this type of computing are discovered, users' computational demands on blockchain networks have become increasingly heterogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-011: Initially, with Bitcoin, computational demands were restricted to simple arithmetic operations as users of the network were primarily interested in making financial transactions.
elements.sent-2411-012: Today, users make increasingly sophisticated computational demands in the form of arbitrary smart contracts.
elements.sent-2411-013: The magnitude and scope of demand for computation continues to expand as more specialized on-chain services are deployed and get traction.
elements.sent-2411-014: In response to this increasing heterogeneity in use cases, protocol designers have sought to develop specialized computational infrastructure to supply this demand.
elements.sent-2411-015: A concrete and recent example of such specialization on the Ethereum blockchain is EIP-4844~\cite{4844 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This proposed change to Ethereum enables a specialized form of short-term storage called ``blobs''.
elements.sent-2411-017: These improvements were designed in response to the computational demands that L2 networks and other services now have (\cite{scroll-costs,zksync,matic .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Numerous sovereign blockchains and rollups themselves also specialize in executing certain types of computational demand (e.g. proving services such as Lagrange\footnote{\url{https://docs.lagrange.dev/zk-coprocessor/overview , Gevulot\footnote{\url{https://gevulot.com/ , SP1\footnote{\url{https://blog.succinct.xyz/introducing-sp1/ , ML and AI training services \cite{bittensor,gensyn-litepaper,modulus , FHE \cite{fhenix-whitepaper, zama-whitepaper , oracle networks \cite{chainlink,gelato ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: As a result, there is increasing heterogeneity not only in demand but also supply of trusted computation.
elements.sent-2411-020: In order to efficiently match computational supply with demand, blockchain networks use market mechanisms that set fees and rewards for users and computational suppliers respectively.
elements.sent-2411-021: Major blockchains including Ethereum measure the resource usage of a given transaction using a single numeraire, and set prices for transaction inclusion by setting a unit price for the numeraire.
elements.sent-2411-022: These fee mechanisms have been studied extensively in recent work, and their implementations (e.g. EIP-1559) satisfy desirable properties.\cite{tfm-1559,tfm-foundations,tfm-collusion In order to efficiently match demand and supply in the presence of heterogeneity, however, these market mechanisms must be adapted.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The Ethereum blockchain, for example, has recently added a second resource numeraire to price the usage of the blobs as part of EIP-4844 \cite{4844 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This general idea of having multiple resource numeraires, or ``dimensions,'' across which the heterogeneity in computational demand is expressed, and subsequently setting a unit price for usage across each numeraire to clear demand is known as a multi-dimensional fee market.
elements.sent-2411-025: Recent work has studied their properties \cite{multidim1,multidim2,chaos,multidim3,multidim4 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Services beyond Ethereum and other L1 networks face more complexity due to greater levels of heterogeneity in demand and supply.
elements.sent-2411-027: Prover networks \cite{MD-for-prover-markets are a notable example, where users have heterogeneous values for proof capabilities of different suppliers, and suppliers have heterogeneous costs and constraints for each transaction due to variability in hardware quality and specialization as well as the ability to parallelize.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Another example is Ritual\footnote{\url{https://ritual.net/ , which seeks to enable users with highly heterogeneous and expensive AI/ML compute demands to execute transactions over a highly heterogeneous collection of compute nodes with different resources.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This paper describes and analyzes a market design problem that generalizes all of these instances, with full heterogeneity on both sides of the market.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our principal contribution in this paper is the description and analysis of the Resonance mechanism, which succeeds even in the presence of full heterogeneity in compute demand and supply.
elements.sent-2411-031: The setting in which this mechanism operates is extremely general.
elements.sent-2411-032: We allow transactions to have arbitrary valuations, and nodes to have arbitrary cost functions over bundles of transactions to be executed.
elements.sent-2411-033: We also allow fully arbitrary constraints on the set of valid allocations.
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition to resource constraints (as mentioned in the previous subsection), we might for example want to ensure that transactions that touch the same part of the network state are run by the same node.
elements.sent-2411-035: Certain nodes may also be incapable of handling certain transactions.
elements.sent-2411-036: All of these restrictions are allowed under our model.
elements.sent-2411-037: One can ask whether existing fee market designs can be extended to this setting.
elements.sent-2411-038: We perform a formal analysis on whether multi-dimensional fee markets can yield good performance in Section \ref{sec:mdfm .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Our results imply that in a heterogeneous setting, selecting allocations and setting prices are both complicated in their own right, even if one has access to an optimal oracle for the other.
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, pricing is challenging because higher heterogeneity necessitates a higher number of prices.
elements.sent-2411-041: This limits the applicability of the currently popular approach of using an on-chain dynamic price oracle that updates according to historical signals about supply and demand.
elements.sent-2411-042: Implementing a pricing oracle at that level of granularity on-chain is infeasible both computationally and information-theoretically.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our high-level solution to these challenges is to off-load both of these complex tasks to a third party of agents that we call brokers.
elements.sent-2411-044: These sophisticated agents are responsible for finding allocations and prices, and in exchange can extract part of the generated surplus from the allocation they find.
elements.sent-2411-045: We show that our mechanism is (a) budget-balanced, (b) individually rational for users, nodes, and brokers, (c) surplus-maximizing in equilibrium, (d) incentive-compatible for users and nodes (conditional on brokers playing certain natural equilibrium strategies), and (e) computationally tractable.
elements.sent-2411-046: The mechanism itself is quite simple.
elements.sent-2411-047: Each broker submits an allocation and individualized payments for all users and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-048: The auctioneer collects the broker submissions, as well as valuations and costs from users and nodes, and finds the surplus maximizing broker submission (as computed given the elicited valuations and costs).
elements.sent-2411-049: If that broker submission satisfies individual rationality for all users and nodes (i.e., results in non-negative utility for them given their elicited valuations/costs), the mechanism outputs the broker's submission, and pays out any leftover cash after settling payments to the chosen broker.
elements.sent-2411-050: The analysis of the mechanism's incentive properties in particular is subtle.
elements.sent-2411-051: Since strong notions such as dominant-strategy incentive-compatibility (DSIC) are impossible to achieve in this setting \cite{myerson1981optimal , we introduce a new version of incentive-compatibility that our mechanism satisfies, which might be of independent interest.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This definition (\cref{def:dsic-barring-s ) is appropriate for a setting where there are two types of agents, ordinary and sophisticated, and the primary objective is to minimize the need for strategization by ordinary agents.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The best-response conditions of the new definition accordingly only checks the best-response conditions of each ordinary agent with respect to alternative actions of other ordinary agents, while keeping keeping the strategies of sophisticated agents fixed.
elements.sent-2411-054: This relaxation of incentive-compatibility can provide an assurance to an ordinary agent that might be considering reasoning about strategies of other ordinary agents that such strategization will not be profitable.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, it provides no guarantees with respect to alternative strategies of sophisticated agents.
elements.sent-2411-056: Assuming ordinary agents have similar levels of rationality and resources, it is plausible that they would entertain the former category of strategization but not the latter.
elements.sent-2411-057: We conclude the paper by listing some practical considerations when deploying Resonance.
elements.sent-2411-058: We give a high level overview of potential extensions to the mechanism that could address these considerations, and defer a more rigorous treatment to future work.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the heterogeneous setting, there are two types of agents, users who demand computation, and nodes who supply it.
elements.sent-2411-060: Each user submits a transaction $t$, which we will use to refer to both the transaction and the user who submitted it.
elements.sent-2411-061: Let $T$ be the set of transactions (and users), $N$ the set of nodes, and $A:=T\cup N$ the set of all agents.
elements.sent-2411-062: For each transaction $t \in T$, there is a valuation $v_t\in\mathbb{R _{\ge0}$ that denotes the maximum amount of money that the user $t$ is willing to pay to have $t$ be executed.}

elements.sent-2411-067: We refer to these quantities as the agents' types, with $\theta_a$ denoting the type of agent $a\in A$ defined as $v_a$ if $a\in T$ and $c_a$ if $a\in N$.

elements.sent-2411-069: An allocation $\alpha:T\rightarrow 2^N$ assigns each transaction to a (possibly empty) set of nodes.

elements.sent-2411-080: A transaction payment rule $\pi: T \to \mathbb R_{\ge 0}$ determines how much each transaction should pay for execution.
elements.sent-2411-081: A node payment rule $\phi: N \to \mathbb R_{\ge 0}$ determines how much each node should be paid for the execution of transactions assigned to it.
elements.sent-2411-082: A routing $(\alpha, \pi, \phi)$ specifies a valid allocation $\alpha \in V$, and the user and node payment rules.
elements.sent-2411-083: Note that that the definition of routings requires that the allocation is valid.
elements.sent-2411-084: We denote by $\mc{R}$ the set of all routings.
elements.sent-2411-085: The margin of a routing $(\alpha, \pi, \phi)$ measures the net cash flow resulting from it, and is given by $\sum_{t\in T}\pi(t)-\sum_{n\in N}\phi(n).$ We denote by $\gamma$ the function that takes as input a routing and outputs its margin.
elements.sent-2411-086: A routing is called budget-balanced if its margin is non-negative.

elements.sent-2411-092: A takeaway from the previous section is that in order to obtain good welfare guarantees while maintaining a simple user experience in a heterogeneous setting, transactions must be priced individually.
elements.sent-2411-093: The natural question is how to discover prices at such a high level of granularity.
elements.sent-2411-094: In particular, existing solutions that implement an on-chain price oracle based on historical demand run into information theoretic and computational issues if extended to provide individualized pricing.
elements.sent-2411-095: This is because the amount of data necessary to learn a high-dimensional price vector is very large, and the aggregation mechanism necessary for an accurate estimator needs to be a sophisticated algorithm that is costly to run on-chain.
elements.sent-2411-096: Can we design a tractable mechanism that works in the fully heterogeneous setting?.
elements.sent-2411-097: The following are properties we desire in a routing mechanism (stated informally here and formalized later).
elements.sent-2411-098: Budget-Balance: The mechanism should not require subsidization to run.
elements.sent-2411-099: Individual Rationality: No one is worse off by participating in the mechanism than abstaining.
elements.sent-2411-100: Efficiency: The mechanism finds a surplus-maximizing routing.
elements.sent-2411-101: Incentive-Compatibility: The mechanism is ex-post incentive compatible, meaning truth-telling by users and nodes is a Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-102: Tractability: The mechanism is computationally lightweight and feasible to implement on-chain.
elements.sent-2411-103: A simple special case of our setting is the problem of bilateral trade, where there is one transaction and one node, and the transaction has value $v$ for being assigned while the node has cost $c$ for executing the transaction.
elements.sent-2411-104: When these values are private, it is well-known that the first four properties cannot be simultaneously satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-105: That is, without private information, surplus-maximization is simply an optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-106: However, even with a perfect price oracle, the computational task of finding an efficient allocation with arbitrary feasibility constraints can be hard to even approximate, failing the tractability requirement.
elements.sent-2411-107: This is in addition to the tractability issues of implementing the price oracle itself.
elements.sent-2411-108: Motivated by these challenges, we propose a mechanism that delegates the complicated tasks of estimating prices and optimal allocations to a third set of agents, called brokers.
elements.sent-2411-109: Brokers specialize in finding efficient routings, and are compensated for their efforts by earning some of the generated surplus, called margin.
elements.sent-2411-110: As a result, the incentives of brokers are aligned with finding the surplus-maximizing allocation, and competition between brokers results in low extracted margins.
elements.sent-2411-111: This effectively implements an allocation and price oracle, which the mechanism uses to achieve efficiency as well as incentive-compatibility for transactions and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-112: While mechanism designers typically strive to minimize the need for participant sophistication, here we only aim to minimize that need for a subset of participants (users and nodes), while relying on sophistication of other participants (brokers) to ease the computational cost of running the mechanism itself.
elements.sent-2411-113: We will next describe the Resonance mechanism, and show that it satisfies all five properties.
elements.sent-2411-114: Resonance is formalized as the following game-form between transactions $T$, nodes $N$, and a set of brokers $B$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We will denote by $A$ the set $A$ of all transactions and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-116: As before, users and nodes have private types representing their payoff functions, with $\theta_a\in\Theta_a$ denoting the type of agent $a\in A$ defined as $v_a$ if $a\in T$ and $c_a$ if $a\in N$.
elements.sent-2411-117: In this paper, we consider a setting where every broker knows the exact types of every transaction and node (and transactions and nodes are aware of this fact).\footnote{See Broker Specialization in \cref{sec:conclusion for a discussion of when brokers have incomplete information.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The mechanism is direct for transactions and nodes, meaning their action spaces are the same as their type spaces.
elements.sent-2411-120: Each agent $a\in A$ submits a type $\theta'_a\in \Theta_a$ to the mechanism (which may not be the same as $a$'s true type $\theta_a$).
elements.sent-2411-121: Each broker $b$ submits a proposal to the mechanism, which consists of a routing $R_b$.
elements.sent-2411-122: We will denote by $\Sigma_b$ the action space of broker $b$, and by $\Sigma=\bigtimes_{b\in B}\Sigma_b$ the action space of all brokers.
elements.sent-2411-123: Resonance is a deterministic map that takes as input an an action profile $(\B\theta',\B\sigma) \in \Theta\times\Sigma$ and outputs a routing $R$ as well as a winning broker $b$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Let $b^*$ be the the broker who submitted the highest-surplus budget-balanced proposal given the submissions of transactions and nodes, that is \begin{equation}b^* := \arg\max_{b\in B}\set{\mc S(R_b\mid \B \theta') \mid  $R_b$ budget-balanced},\end{equation} breaking ties according to an arbitrary fixed ordering over the brokers (that does not depend on the actions of any participants).
elements.sent-2411-125: Let $R^*$ be the routing submitted by $b^*$.
elements.sent-2411-126: If $R^*$ results in negative utility for any transaction or node assuming true types $\B\theta'$, (i.e. if there exists some some $a\in A$ such that $u(a,R^*\mid \theta_a)< 0$), then the mechanism outputs the empty routing.
elements.sent-2411-128: Payments to users and nodes are settled according to $R^*$.
elements.sent-2411-129: Payments to brokers are set to zero if the mechanism outputs the empty routing.
elements.sent-2411-130: Otherwise, payments are set to zero for all $b\not= b^*$, and to the margin of $R^*$ for $b^*$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Users and nodes are quasilinear, and brokers are revenue-maximizing.
elements.sent-2411-132: The utility of a user or node $a$ with type $\theta_a$ under action profile $(\B\theta',\B\sigma) \in \Theta\times\Sigma$ is denoted by $u(a,\B\theta',\B\sigma\mid \theta_a)$ and is defined as their utility under the output routing when everyone plays $(\B\theta',\B\sigma)$.
elements.sent-2411-133: The utility of a broker $b$ under $(\B\theta',\B\sigma)$ is denoted by $u(b,\B\theta',\B\sigma)$ and is equal to the payment it receive from the mechanism when everyone plays $(\B\theta',\B\sigma)$.

elements.sent-2411-146: A common metric for the efficiency of an outcome is the sum of utilities of all participants.
elements.sent-2411-147: Formally, the welfare $\mathcal W$ of an action profile $(\B\theta',\B\sigma)$ under the type vector $\B\theta$ is \begin{equation}\label{eq:welfare} \mathcal W(\B\theta',\B\sigma \mid \B\theta) := \sum_{a\in A} u(a,\B\theta',\B\sigma\mid \theta_a) + \sum_{b\in B} u(b,\B\theta',\B\sigma)\end{equation} Since Resonance is strongly budget balanced and all participants are quasilinear, this is equal to the sum of valuations (minus costs) of all allocated transactions and nodes.

elements.sent-2411-334: We consider a hierarchy of allocations:.
elements.sent-2411-335: In an inclusion-maximal allocation, the mechanism allocates a maximal set of transactions to nodes.

elements.sent-2411-339: In a fee-maximal allocation, the mechanism allocates transactions that maximize the total revenue from base fees.
elements.sent-2411-340: Computing a multi-dimensional fee-maximal allocation involves solving the multiple subset-sum problem, which is NP-Hard, and further hard to approximate \cite{subset-sum .}

elements.sent-2411-448: As a consequence, when nodes have heterogeneous costs, it is necessary to set individual prices for transactions and nodes.

elements.sent-2411-450: This paper introduces Resonance: a new kind of transaction fee mechanism for the general two-sided market setting (with users on one side and nodes on the other), where both sides of the market exhibit a high degree of heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-451: We allow users submitting transactions to have arbitrary valuations for inclusion, nodes responsible for executing transactions to incur arbitrary costs for running any bundle of transactions, and further allow for arbitrary additional constraints on what allocations are valid.
elements.sent-2411-452: These constraints can, for example, be used to prevent state conflicts by requiring transactions that utilize the same part of the network's state to not be executed in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-453: They also enable support for new transaction types, such as transactions that require multiple nodes for execution (e.g. to run multi-party computation for better transaction privacy).
elements.sent-2411-454: Resonance's design utilizes competition among sophisticated brokers to find individualized prices for each transaction and node.
elements.sent-2411-455: These improvements were designed in response to the computational demands that rollup networks and other services now have (\cite{scroll-costs,zksync,matic ).}
elements.sent-2411-456: Numerous sovereign blockchains and rollups themselves also specialize in executing certain types of computational demand (e.g. proving services such as Lagrange\footnote{\url{https://docs.lagrange.dev/zk-coprocessor/overview , Gevulot\footnote{\url{https://gevulot.com/ , and SP1\footnote{\url{https://blog.succinct.xyz/introducing-sp1/ , ML and AI training services \cite{bittensor,gensyn-litepaper,modulus , FHE \cite{fhenix-whitepaper, zama-whitepaper , oracle networks \cite{chainlink,gelato ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: The Ethereum blockchain, for example, has recently added a second resource numeraire to price the usage of blobs as part of EIP-4844 \cite{4844 .}
elements.sent-2411-458: Services beyond Ethereum and other layer one blockchains face even more complexity due to greater levels of heterogeneity in demand and supply.
elements.sent-2411-459: Prover networks \cite{MD-for-prover-markets,lagrange-paper are a notable example, where users have heterogeneous values for proof capabilities of different suppliers, and suppliers have heterogeneous costs and constraints for each transaction due to variability in hardware quality and specialization as well as the ability to parallelize.}
elements.sent-2411-460: Another example is Ritual\footnote{\url{https://ritual.net/ , which seeks to enable users demanding highly heterogeneous and expensive AI and ML compute to execute transactions over a highly heterogeneous collection of compute nodes with different resources.}}
elements.sent-2411-461: Our principal contribution in this paper is the description and analysis of the Resonance mechanism, which succeeds in an extremely general setting, with fully heterogeneous supply and demand and arbitrary additional constraints on the allocation.
elements.sent-2411-462: To describe our model at a high level, we consider a two-sided marketplace, with users demanding computation on one side and nodes supplying computational resources on the other.
elements.sent-2411-463: Each user submits a transaction, and has an arbitrary (private) value for that transaction getting executed.
elements.sent-2411-464: Each node has an arbitrary (private) cost function for executing any bundle of transactions.
elements.sent-2411-465: We define an allocation as an assignment of each transaction to a set of nodes, and allow for an arbitrary validity constraint on the set of ``allowable'' allocations.
elements.sent-2411-466: These constraints can for example ensure that transactions that touch the same part of the network state are run by the same node, or state that nodes are not assigned transactions that exceed their capacity.
elements.sent-2411-467: This setting is very general, and in particular captures previous work on fee mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-468: For example, if there is only one node, which has zero cost for running transactions, and the validity constraint can be expressed as a capacity constraint on a single numeraire (i.e. an allocation is valid if the total gas consumption of allocated transactions does not exceed the maximum block size), we recover the setting of Ethereum fee markets with a single dimension.
elements.sent-2411-469: As another example, if again there is a single numeraire (denoting CPU cycles), but more than one node, and the cost function of each node $n$ is a node-specific constant $x_n$ times the total units of the numeraire assigned to that node, then we recover the setting of prover markets as studied in \cite{MD-for-prover-markets,lagrange-paper .}
elements.sent-2411-470: A fee mechanism in this setting must determine an allocation of transactions to nodes, and prices paid by each transactions as well as paid to each node.
elements.sent-2411-471: Users and nodes have quasilinear utilities, meaning users maximize their value minus payment, and nodes maximize their reward minus cost.
elements.sent-2411-472: The goal is to design a mechanism that satisfies several desirable properties (stated informally):.
elements.sent-2411-473: Efficiency: The mechanism must maximize surplus, defined as the sum of utilities of users and nodes.

elements.sent-2411-477: We formally define the five properties for a setting with brokers as well as users and nodes, and show that the Resonance Mechanism satisfies all properties: It is (a) budget-balanced, (b) individually rational for users, nodes, and brokers, (c) surplus-maximizing in equilibrium, (d) incentive-compatible for users and nodes (conditional on brokers playing certain natural equilibrium strategies), and (e) computationally tractable.
